text: This fifty nine years old female presented with lower abdominal discomfort and off and on blood in the stool. On colonoscopy she was detected to have multiple taberticula limited to the sigmoid colon with signs of inflammation.
Plan was laparoscopic sigmoidectomy.
The surgeon isolates and clips the sigmoid branches of the inferior mesenteric artery close to the sigmoid colon during the laparoscopic sigmoidectomy procedure.

In the left lower quadrant of the abdomen, adhesions due to diverticulitis are visible. The small bowel is adherent to the sigmoid colon and the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon carefully separates the adhesions while maintaining hemostasis.
The surgeon separates the small bowel from the anterior abdominal wall and detaches it from the inflamed sigmoid colon.

The surgeon carefully separates the sigmoid colon from the anti-abdominal wall, encountering thick fibrous adhesions. The adhesions are meticulously dissected to avoid opening the sigmoid colon and prevent fecal contamination.
The surgeon separates inflamed diverticula from the sigmoid colon, with the tinea coli visibly running along the length of the colon.

The surgeon dissects the right leaf of the mesosigmoid, identifying the sacral promontory and dividing the leaf of the mesocolon above it, following the release of the sigmoid colon from the anti-abdominal wall.

The surgeon continues the downward division of tissue, aiming to identify the sigmoid branches of the inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection is performed carefully and slowly to reveal these vascular structures.

The surgeon identifies the first sigmoid branch of the inferior mesenteric artery, pushing the artery downwards for better exposure. The branch is cleared of surrounding tissue, clipped, and divided using harmonic shears. The remaining two sigmoid branches are similarly isolated, identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon continues the dissection downwards to the rectosigmoid junction, exposing the posterior wall of the rectosigmoid. The mesosigmoid is dissected up to the third piece of the sacrum, while preserving the inferior mesenteric artery to maintain blood supply to the upper rectum.

The surgeon ensures the caudal end of the dissection includes all diverticula, then turns the peritoneal incision anteriorly and to the left to divide the mesocolon. The muscular fibers of the anterior surface of the rectosigmoid are visible, along with the pulsations of the preserved inferior mesenteric artery. Attention is then directed to the peritoneum on the left lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the rectosigmoid junction and carries the incision upwards along the left paracolic gutter, mobilizing the lower portion of the left colon. The dissection is then directed downwards, with identification of the gonadal vessels and careful safeguarding of the left ureter.
The surgeon observes the peristalsis of the ureter, which is visible as rhythmic contractions along the ureter's length.

The surgeon continues the incision on the left side of the rectosigmoid towards the pelvis, joining the previous incision made from the right side. The posterior surface of the rectosigmoid is now fully mobilized and free from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses an Echelon 60 stapler with a green load to divide the rectosigmoid after it has been circumferentially dissected and all surrounding fat has been removed.

The surgeon exteriorizes the colon through an incision in the left iliac fossa. The excised specimen is removed, and the anvil of the stapler is placed. A purse string suture is applied, and the colon is pushed inward.
A 33 French SDH stapler is introduced trans-anally, with the spike advancing through the staple line. The rectal wall is visibly supported as the spike penetrates.

The surgeon locks the anvil of the stapler at the sigmoid colon and approximates the ends of the sigmoid and rectum by closing the stapler. Care is taken to ensure no fat or intestine is trapped between the two ends. The stapler line is confirmed to be in the green range before activating the stapler to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon gradually removes the stapler from the anastomosis site. Saline is injected from below to test the integrity of the anastomosis, ensuring there are no leaks.
